chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Hello everybody, thanks for watching this presentation. This presentation covers the topics for chapter 4,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: but with a lot of extra detail about how to write numerically precise code in the context that come up in deep learning.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: For people who are watching this on the book website, you should understand that I'm going to be taking live questions from AI with the best audience members periodically.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And I'll read the questions as I answer them. So I'm going to start out by looking at the slides,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: but I'm going to tab back and forth periodically to check for questions from the audience.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So go ahead and ask questions as they come up and I'll try to answer them quickly.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Today's topic is numerical computation for deep learning.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Basic idea of numerical computation is that we need to be very careful about the way that we handle real numbers in a few different contexts.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: One is if we use an algorithm that has to do some kind of, you know, careful representation of real numbers with a finite number of bits.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Real numbers like pi have infinitely many digits. And then when we represent them in a computer, we usually use 32 bit floating point numbers as an approximation for real numbers when we when we implemented deep learning algorithm on GPU.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The use of 32 bits to represent something that really has infinitely many digits can often cause errors.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And we'd like to try to minimize the amount of damage to our program that happens when we use that representation.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Another consideration for numerical computation is how sensitive a function is to very small changes in its input.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: This comes up even if we were able to theoretically use real numbers without having to represent them in 32 bits.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: When we do things like optimize a function, if that function is very sensitive to little tiny changes in the input, it becomes a lot harder to search for the inputs that minimize that function.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Because little tiny changes in our search can strongly influence the outcome of the search.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: As a brief roadmap for today, I'm going to cover two different topics.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The first is iterative optimization. How we actually make algorithms that search for the minimal point of some function.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And how this gets affected by numerical considerations.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Next, I'm going to talk about rounding error underflow and overflow.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: How the use of 32 bits to represent real numbers can affect deep learning programs.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So starting out with iterative optimization, we're going to cover three different topics here.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The first is how the gradient descent algorithm works.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The second is curvature. The idea that the functions are optimizing are nonlinear.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And how the second order curvature of these functions affects the optimization process.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And then finally, we'll have a few comments on constrained optimization.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The basic algorithm that we use for most iterative optimization is gradient descent.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And I've illustrated this here with a really simple function.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: F of x equals 1 half x squared.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: You can see it plotted with the dashed blue line that I'm highlighting with my mouse cursor.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If we would like to minimize this function, we can do it by following the gradient.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Let's say that x is greater than 0.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The gradient, in this case, because the function is one-dimensional, is just the derivative.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So the derivative can be represented by the value f prime of x.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Over here, f prime of x, represented by the green line, is positive.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So if we descend the gradient by moving in the opposite direction of the derivative,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: everywhere that we're over here where the green line is positive,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: gradient descent will move left and downhill.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So starting here, we would trace the blue curve leftward until we reach its minimum point here.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Where the derivative is 0, we are no longer told by the derivative to move in any particular

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: direction. So we stop.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If we started over here on the left side, over here, the derivative is negative.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And we move and opposite the direction of the derivative.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So we would actually apply positive updates to our input x.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And we would move rightward along the function x squared

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: until we reach the minimum and stop where the derivative is equal to 0.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: I've described the gradient descent basically in terms of this idea that we

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: move downhill until we find a point where the derivative is 0.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And the example of f of x equals 1 half x squared, there was only one point like that.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In reality, there might be a lot of different points that have zero derivatives.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Here I've drawn a function that's very squiggly and has several different local minima.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Local minima are points that are smaller than in f of x value than all the other points surrounding

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: them, but they're not necessarily the lowest point overall.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So here, if we look to the left or the right of this point, we go uphill a little bit.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If you go further to the left, we can find much lower points.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Usually in the context of deep learning, we can't really expect to find this global minimum over here.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: We might want to do that, but it's probably not feasible.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In practice, we often give up and find a point more like this one here.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: It's a local minimum, but the value of f of x is actually very low.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So we've done a good job of reducing the value of the function,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: even if we don't find our way all the way to the bottom of the function.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In practice, we don't even necessarily find a local minimum.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In practice, we often just sort of get stuck for numerical reasons, and that's the topic of this chapter.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: I'm fast forwarding a little bit, and I'm showing you a figure from chapter 8.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: On the x-axis, I show you how long gradient descent has been running on a particular problem.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In the two different plots, I show you two different things.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: On the left side, I show you how big the gradients are over time,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: specifically I'm plotting the norm of the gradient.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Every little black dot is a different step of the gradient descent algorithm.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: You can see it's kind of noisy.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: On different steps, it's not like we always see the same gradient norm twice in a row.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: It bounces around a lot.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: But I've fit a smooth blue curve to these lines.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: You can see how the gradient norm actually gradually gets bigger over time.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Based on the plots that I showed you on the previous slides,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: you might expect that the gradient norm would shrink as you approach a local minimum

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: and it can graduate to go down to zero.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Well, it turns out that a lot of the time,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: we actually just get stuck and can't really keep making progress toward regions of smaller

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: gradient norm.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And so we stop when f of x has become very small,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: rather than when the gradient has gone away.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: You could say maybe this plot that I made here where I show the gradient getting bigger over time,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: maybe that's an example of failure, maybe the algorithm is just broken.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: But actually, if we look at the plot on the right,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: I'm showing you the error rate of a classifier that we're training with gradient descent.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And this error rate decreases over time successfully and levels off to about state of the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: art accuracy for the problem that this is training on.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So you can see that this is actually an example of the optimization algorithm

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: succeeding from our point of view.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: It's not actually finding a local minimum.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: It's just finding a very low value of the function f of x that are minimizing.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And that low value turns out to be good enough for us to use the model.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Someone has asked, why don't we test a larger diversity when we've reached a local minimum?

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Wouldn't that tell us we could go to lower elsewhere?

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And the answer is yes, a lot of the time we do things like run multiple random

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: restarts of the optimization algorithm.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And sometimes you can find lower values that way.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: For a lot of problems, you also find that several different random restarts

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: end up with about the same final value.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Someone else asked, can you please explain gradient norm a bit?

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The basic idea is if you have an input to your function x with multiple coordinates,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: then the gradient gives you the derivative of your function with respect to each of those

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: coordinates.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And that tells us the direction we should go in to move downhill.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If you measure the norm of that gradient, that gives you an idea of how steep the function is.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: It tells you how quickly you can expect the function to drop if you move in a specific direction.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: I'm going to skip the next question because it's answered later in the talk.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And then there's one more question about how can the gradient increase when you are using a descent

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: direction? Is it not convex?

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And it turns out that the gradient can increase if you optimize a neural net because the function

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: is not convex. It's also possible that if you use too large of a step size, the gradient can

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: increase because you move to a region that's steeper.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: I think of the way that we optimize deep learning functions as more of a way of life than

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: a strictly mathematical discipline right now.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If you approach optimization from a really pure math point of view,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: you're usually looking to do something like find literally the smallest value of some function f of x.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Or you might have some other formally defined criterion like you're looking for a critical point

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: of f of x where the value is locally the smallest.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In some neighborhood. For deep learning, we usually have a more qualitative pragmatic approach

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: where our goal is to just decrease the value of f of x a lot.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And we aren't necessarily hoping to actually find even a local minimum.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: We just want to make the error rate go down until the error rate is low enough that our model can

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: be used for some engineering application. Actually finding a critical point might be very difficult.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And a lot of the critical points we find may not be what we want.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The next topic in our overview of iterative optimization is the effective curvature on these

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: optimization algorithms. We've mentioned that gradient descent in theory will approach a critical

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: point where the gradient is equal to zero. Here I show you three different kinds of critical points

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: in one dimension. There can be a minimum where the function curves upward around the critical point

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: and the critical point is thus lower in value than all of the surrounding points.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: There can also be a local maximum where the function curves downward so the critical point is higher

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: in value than all the surrounding points. Or you can have saddle points where you actually are at

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: neither a minimum nor a maximum. Settle points make a little bit more sense in two dimensions.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: You can think of it as there's one axis where the critical point is a local minimum and another axis.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Where the critical point is a local maximum.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Local maximum are usually not much of a problem for optimization algorithms. The saddle points can be

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: a problem for iterative optimization algorithms. There's one algorithm called Newton's method

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: where the basic strategy is to solve for a point where the gradient is equal to zero.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If you're able to solve that problem algebraically you can jump to a solution right away.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Unfortunately a solution in terms of Newton's method is not always actually a minimum.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If you have a function that has saddle points in it these are also places where the gradient is zero

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: but they are not really a minimum because they're a maximum across some axes.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So algorithms like Newton's method will accidentally teleport onto saddle points and get stuck.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: It turns out that for neural networks there are a lot more saddle points than local minima.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Fortunately saddle points are not a big problem for gradient descent.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Unless you initialize directly on the saddle point gradient descent can escape the saddle point.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If you look at appendix c of a paper called qualitatively characterizing neural network optimization

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: problems I have some math there where I show why gradient descent is able to escape.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: We can characterize all of these different critical points by using curvature. As I said a local

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: minimum is where the function curves up around the point and the local maximum is where it curves down.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: We can also think of this as a way of predicting how well gradient descent will perform.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Gradient descent basically says we're going to look at the slope of the function and we're going to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: pretend that the function is linear nearby our current x value and then we're going to move in a

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: direction that will make our linear approximation go downhill. The amount of actual payoff that you

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: get from that depends on how curved the function is. If your function is perfectly linear

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: if you make a linear approximation to the function then it will just overlay the function exactly.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So here you can see I've got this dashed green line overlaying the blue line.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If we take a little step according to the direction of the derivative we're going to decrease the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: function f of x by exactly the same amount that our linear function predicted and that means that

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: these iterative search algorithms where we always just take little steps moving downhill

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: are going to work really well regardless of the size of step that we use.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If we have negative curvature where the function curves downward as we move away from the current

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: point then we're actually in a very nice situation where if we take a step that was predicted to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: reduce the dashed green linear function approximating f of x we'll find that f of x actually decreases

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: even more than our approximation predicted it to do. So if we can find directions of negative

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: curvature they're a good way to reduce the value of the function very quickly. Unfortunately what

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: happens a lot more often is we have directions of positive curvature. With directions of positive

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: curvature the function curves up relative to the linear function. So when you take small steps

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: we end up not nearly decreasing the function as much as we wanted to and in fact if we take two

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: big of a step we can accidentally increase the function value we can overshoot the minimum in this

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: direction and go back uphill. That's why we start looking at what's called the numerics of the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: function. We look at how much curvature there is to this function and see how it affects our ability

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: to take large steps to search for the minimum value of the function. Someone asked what was the name

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: of the paper that I just told you can actually see that in the slides they're available at deep

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: learning book.org. So far I've shown you these one-dimensional examples of curvature but in practice

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: x is an n-dimensional vector and we need to understand how the curvature affects the function in

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: many different directions. We can do that using the Hessian matrix. The Hessian matrix is a matrix

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: containing all of the second derivatives. So if you look at rho i and column j of the Hessian

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: matrix you'll find the derivative where first we take the derivative with respect to x i and

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: next we take the derivative with respect to x j. It turns out that you can use the Hessian matrix

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: to compute the second derivative in a particular direction d. You can do that by computing d transpose

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: hd where d is a unit vector describing the direction where you would like to get the second

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: derivative. It turns out that there's actually some nice intuition for what second derivative you'll

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: get. Hessian matrices are usually symmetric assuming that different properties of the function

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: are held and a symmetric real valued matrix has an eigen decomposition in terms of orthogonal

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: eigenvectors and eigenvalues associated with each eigenvector. So you can write all of the eigenvectors

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: in a matrix q and they become this orthogonal basis for the space that you're in. And then you

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: have a diagonal matrix of eigenvalues. If we look back at some of the slides from chapter 2 we can see

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: what happens when we multiply by a matrix that has particular eigenvectors and eigenvalues.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: If we start out with a bunch of points that are represented by this blue circle here and we

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: multiply each of these points by a matrix that has eigenvectors v1 and v2. We'll see that each of

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the points get stretched away from the origin according to the eigenvalues of the matrix. So if you

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: have a large lambda 1 eigenvalue associated with v1 you'll actually stretch out the circle in this

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: direction. Points will get pulled further out by a factor of lambda 1. And then if you have a small

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: eigenvalue associated with direction v2 points will get pulled inward. So you can see that these

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: directions will be when you multiply h by d, d will either get stretched or shrunk according to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: how big or small the eigenvalues of the Hessian are in that direction. And then when you take the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: dot product with the direction again you'll end up just measuring the size of that vector.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So you'll end up saying that the second derivative is based on how much the Hessian shrinks

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: or stretches your vector in that particular direction. But what happens if your direction is not

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: an eigenvector? Well then it turns out that you basically interpolate based on the eigenvectors

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that are nearby. You can compute the second derivative as a sum over all the different eigenvalues.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And you multiply by this term that measures how similar the direction you're looking in is to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that corresponding eigenvalue. You can kind of see that in this plot that if you have a direction

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that's very closely aligned with eigenvector 2 you're going to get your directions stretched by

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: approximately eigenvalue 2. And then as you move towards eigenvalue 1 the amount that your

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: direction gets stretched is going to scale gradually up to the amount of stretching that comes from

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: eigenvalue 1. You're never going to stretch more than the maximum eigenvalue. You're never going to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: stretch less than the minimum eigenvalue. We can use these eigenvalues to figure out what the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: optimal step size is likely to be. So first off if we have a direction of negative curvature

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: even a second order approximation would tell us that we can keep stepping forever.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: That's probably not going to be true for a neural net because it's very non-linear, very,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: it's not even really quadratic. It's likely to start going back uphill at some point for a large

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: step size. The main place where the Hessian tells us a lot of information is if we look at the case

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: of positive curvature. We know that if we start following the linear function downhill we can

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: decrease the value of the function. But if we step too far we'll start to go back uphill. We don't

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: want to step any further than this minimum point here. We can make a second order Taylor series

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: approximation to the value that we'll get when we take a specific gradient step. We can say we're

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: starting off at some point x0 and we're taking a gradient descent step where we move by step size

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: epsilon multiplied by a gradient g. The first order Taylor series approximation, or sorry,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: second order Taylor series approximation tells us that the value at this new point will be

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: approximately equal to the value at our current point minus epsilon times the squared norm of the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: gradient. This is the first order Taylor series term plus one half epsilon squared times the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: gradient multiplied by the Hessian multiplied by the gradient. This here is the directional second

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: derivative in the direction of the gradient but also scaled by the size of the gradient.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So if we then solve this equation for the minimum predicted value that we'll get after the step

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: and we solve it in terms of the step size assuming that we're looking in a direction of

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: positive curvature, we find that the optimal step size is equal to the squared norm of the gradient

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: divided by g transpose hg. So what does this tell us overall? The numerator says that if the gradient

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: is bigger, we can take bigger steps. So big gradients will speed up the optimization algorithm.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In the denominator, we actually see that this g transpose hg term can shrink the optimal step size a

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: lot. So if the gradient happens to point in a direction that's aligned with an eigenvector of the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Hessian that has a big eigenvalue, you have to slow down a lot. Otherwise, we'll overshoot the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: minimum and go back up hill. Overall, this shows us something about why when we actually run an

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: iterative search in practice, we may not actually arrive at a critical point. Over time, we'd expect the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: gradient to get smaller and smaller, but we'll often also find for the kinds of optimization problems

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that come up with neural nets. We'll find that this g transpose hg term gets very big and we're just

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: not able to make very big steps anymore, even if the gradient remains large. We can measure this with

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: something called the condition number. The condition number looks at the largest ratio we can find

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: between two of our eigenvalues. If there's a very large eigenvalue and a very small eigenvalue,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: it means that when our gradient points in the direction of the large eigenvalue, we're forced to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: take very small steps. And we have to do that even if sometimes the gradient would point in the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: direction of a small eigenvalue and it would be safe to take larger steps. Basically, if there's a wide

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: range of eigenvalues, gradient descent has to miss out on a lot of the potential value that it could

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: find on each step. It has to keep the step size small to avoid accidentally going up.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: You can see this with this contour plot. Here we're minimizing a quadratic function.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Each of the black rings shows a set of points where the function is of equal value. So at the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: middle here, we've got a minimum of the function. Every time we see a ring on our way out, we're taking

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: a step up in value of the function. And the red path is showing how gradient descent moves.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Ideally, gradient descent would want to just point straight at the minimum and go straight there.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Instead, we keep finding that the gradients tend to point across this canyon. And so we keep going

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: down the canyon wall, overshooting the bottom of the canyon, and coming to the other side of the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: canyon. So you're going this zigzagging fashion where we're always walking up or down a canyon wall

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: instead of kind of flowing down along the bottom of the canyon. You can see this with neural networks

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that same paper that I mentioned earlier includes this visualization of a little 3D

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: representation of what happens in a neural net optimization problem with millions of parameters.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: We can see that toward the end of training, gradient descent, as represented by this blue path,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: is moving down this really tight canyon with the green 3D mesh. And so the gradient is actually

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: still pretty large here. But the curvature is even larger. And the curvature makes it really hard to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: take large steps. Some things about this visualization are a little bit hard to understand because

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: when you work with multiple dimensions and project them down to just a few dimensions,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: some things can be lost. So one thing that we can't see here is that this blue curve is actually

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: zigzagging a lot, just like in the previous visualization. It's just that the way that we

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: made this plot means that we can't actually see the other dimensions where it's zigzagging.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: But those zigzags are stopping it from moving a lot faster along the bottom of this canyon.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The last topic that we'll cover for iterative optimization is the idea of constrained optimization.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And I don't actually have a lot to say about this. I'm just going to very quickly mention

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: a little bit about how it works. If you have constraints for a function,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: like if you have some values G that measure some how much you deviate from exactly satisfying a

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: constraint or some values H that measure how far you are on the wrong side of some inequality,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: you can actually take your original function f of x and transform it into this new function called

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the Lagrangian function where you penalize the function for violating those constraints.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And you can then solve instead of just a minimization problem, you can solve a minmax problem

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: where you want to keep increasing the coefficients on those penalty terms until you satisfy the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: penalty terms. This will give you a solution to the constrained optimization problem rather than

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: just letting you search over all of space. In this book, we usually use this Lagrangian or

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: this is also called the KKT version when you include both the exact and the inequality constraints.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In this book, we mostly use this approach for solving problems in theory. Like if we want to show

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that the Gaussian is the probability distribution of the highest entropy, we can use some of these

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: penalty terms to constrain that the function we're searching over is a probability distribution that's

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: non-negative and sums to one. In practice, if we actually want to write a program that we run where we

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: use several iterations to search for a solution, there's a much easier thing you can do. Instead of

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: having this minmax problem in Lagrangian penalties and so on, you can just project x back to the nearest

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: point that satisfies the constraints after each step. And that's a very practical, easy to implement

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: way of solving these kinds of problems. Someone has asked which data visualization did you use to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: plot the data points? And for that, you should read the paper. It's a visualization that became

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: up specifically for that paper. It's not a standard technique. Someone else asked what happens if we

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: do not choose the direction where the gradient points, but select the direction where the step we can

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: take is maximal. So yeah, you can actually look for directions where you intentionally seek out

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: negative curvature and so on. It turns out to be difficult to do that in high-dimensional space.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Someone asks what gradient to set up the Mises would you recommend? I would say usually Adam or

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: gradient descent with momentum are pretty good, and a lot of the time you should consider using

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: gradient clipping. The next part of our overall roadmap is to discuss what happens when we have

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: rounding error. When we have underflow, overflow, all of these issues that happen when we use a finite

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: number of bits to represent real numbers. This is really important. I know it sounds kind of boring to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: talk about this low-level stuff with bits, but writing numerically precise code is actually a deep

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: learning super skill. A lot of the time you'll find that deep learning algorithms sort of work when

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: you code them up. The loss will go down, the accuracy will go up, the accuracy will get within a

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: few percentage points of the state of the art. You can check your code really carefully and you can

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: find that there aren't really any bugs, but maybe there are some things that aren't quite optimal

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: about it. That's often caused by a loss of numerical precision where you run your gradient descent

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: algorithm and you don't get quite of good as results as you think you should. Another time you'll find

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that your deep learning algorithm explodes. You get a lot of not a number of values, you get a lot of

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: very large values, and this might happen even if you make your learning rate relatively small and

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: you think everything should be stable. Both of these problems actually usually come from writing code

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that doesn't have high enough numerical precision. Being able to write numerically precise code can

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: really differentiate how well you perform as a deep learning practitioner. A lot of people have heard

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the story of how when I first thought of generative adversarial nets, I was at a bar and I went

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: home and I coded them up immediately and within an hour or so later I actually had them working.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: A lot of people have wondered how I was able to do that and part of the answer is that I knew about

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: all of this really boring numerical precision stuff. So I didn't waste any time shooting myself in

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the foot writing numerically and precise code and that reduces the amount of time it takes to get

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the algorithm working a lot. The basic idea is that when you use a finite number of bits to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: represent a real number, you get both rounding errors and what are called truncation errors.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: In a digital computer we're usually talking about using float32 or some other similar schemes

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: to represent real numbers. That's especially true when we're using CUDA on GPUs. Sometimes we use

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: other floating formats. A lot of the problems I'm going to describe here will often mostly go away

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: if you're able to switch to float64 but that usually requires a slower computation on GPU

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: or may even force you to move to CPU. It will also force you to use more memory. So if you can write

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: numerically precise code using float32, then that can be a big advantage. There's basically three

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: categories of problems that happen on these kinds of 32-bit representations. So first, most numbers

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that you work with, they're not going to cause catastrophic problems on their own, but they are

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: going to get rounded. If you have some real number x and you turn it into a representation on computer,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: it's going to get rounded to some value x plus delta where delta is just some small value

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: describing the rounding error that happened that you'll be off by a little bit from what you meant to be.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: A larger problem is when you have some extremely large value of x, you just can't represent it with

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the range of numbers that your float32 values can represent. And it will actually get replaced by

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: some placeholder, like, inf, just saying that x is too large to represent. And so now it's not

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: really a finite value anymore. You've got this infinite number and your code will probably still go

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: ahead and keep doing arithmetic on this number as if it were a real value and then you'll get in trouble.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: There's another topic which is still basically a kind of rounding error, but it's a particularly

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: harmful kind of rounding error. If you have a very small value of x, it can get replaced by zero.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: This is especially harmful because a lot of the algorithms in deep learning often assume that we

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: have some very small but non-zero variable. Like, for example, if you use the logistic

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: sigmoid activation function, you can pass very negative values to the logistic sigmoid.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And algebraically, they'll never become quite zero, but in a computer, they can definitely get

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: rounded down to zero. And so people are asking, aren't these numerical issues already addressed in

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the current deep learning libraries? And the answer is basically, yes, but you need to know when to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: look out for them. A lot of the time you write your own code and you might end up rewriting a

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: version of something that already has an numerically stable variant. Sometimes you might need to,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: for example, compute a softmax over several tensors instead of just one tensor. And you'll need to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: know how to do that in an numerically stable way if your library only provides a softmax for a single

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: tensor. Some other people are asking if there's any pointers on the best way to deal with nants,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: that's basically what the next several slides will do. Someone asked, how does this 64-bit

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: representation solve the nane error? Well, the basic idea is that if you have 32 bits, there's

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: a certain range of values that you can represent. If you have 64 bits, that range expands.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So a program that might cause a nane when you use 32 bits might actually void the nane when you

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: use 64 bits. If you use a program that uses even bigger numbers, you can eventually still get nants

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: for 64 bits. It's just that fewer programs cause problems using 64 bits than using 32.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: One example is if you look at the Pylon 2 repository that I used to work on in grad school before

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: we had tensor flow, you can look at some of the unit tests and when I compute the KL divergence

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: between two Gaussian distributions, if I use float 64, the unit test expects the KL divergence to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: have a minimum value of 0. But if I use float 32, the unit test actually has to allow the KL

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: divergence to go slightly negative. So one example of how we can get rounding errors and they can

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: affect this code is what happens when you add a very small number to a larger number. So we're going

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: to make an array using 32-bit floats and we're going to store the value 0 and the value 10 to the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: minus 8 in this array. If we compute the argmax of this array, the argmax is 1 because position 0

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: has a value of 0, position 1 has a positive but very small value of 10 to the minus 8. So now

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: suppose that we take these numbers and we add them to a much larger number, we add them to 1.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Well, 0 is going to have 1 added to it and end up with a value of 1. Ideally, 1 plus 10 to the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: minus 8 would be represented as 1 but it's at one point, you know, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1 after the correct

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: number of 0's. In this case, it's actually going to get rounded to 1, which isn't quite what we want.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: When you take the argmax of the newer array where we added 1 to it, we actually get that the argmax

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: is 0 now because NumPy breaks ties to the first index that's tied for the max. So this edition of

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: a number that caused rounding error has actually downstream changed the results quite a lot.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: We only really wanted to extract a single bit from this computation and we're now actually getting

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the value of that bit wrong. There's some secondary effects that happen when we have rounding error

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: and overflow and underflow errors. What happens is after the first error, we do some more math and then

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the second error causes a bigger problem. So for example, so we have some code that computes x minus y

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: x minus y is a relatively safe thing to do to start with. There's definitely some ways that can go

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: wrong but there's not anything fundamentally wrong with doing a subtraction. But now suppose that

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: x overflows to nth, y overflows to nth, well then we've got infinity minus infinity and we don't

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: really know which infinity is larger. This kind of operation is not really defined. So now our x minus

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: y will turn these nths into nths. A lot of the time when you have overflow, the way that you first

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: see it is that you'll get a nth in your loss function or your gradient. So somewhere in your

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: computational graph, you'll get an nth and then by the time it actually gets printed out or plotted

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: somewhere, it will have been turned into a nth by these additional steps of arithmetic that came

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: afterward. One of the main functions that's often the culprit of numerical precision problems in

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: deep learning programs is the x function that computes the natural logarithms base e raised to

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the power of x. This will overflow for large values of x. And a lot of the time when I say large

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: values, I am talking about extremely large values. For x, when I say large values, I'm actually talking

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: about numbers that are not very big. If you're using float 32, a value of 89 past 2x will overflow to nth.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: To make sure that your program doesn't have trouble with x, just never use large x as the argument

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: for x. And make sure that you design the structure of your functions that you compute so that they will

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: never actually pass a large x to x. A lot of the rest of this lecture will be about ideas for how you

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: can prevent that from happening in specific cases. The other big problem with x is that if your argument

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: to it is very negative, x of x can underflow. This may not be a problem. If you think about it,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: x of a very negative number is just some small number epsilon. And if you round that number down to zero,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: you've only made a mistake of size epsilon. So if that was your final output of the function,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: it's not a very big error. Unfortunately, if you then do other math on top of this output,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: rounding a small number epsilon down to zero can be catastrophic. If you're using x of x in

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: a denominator or as an argument to a logarithm, it can cause really big problems.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Another case where you can sometimes encounter danger is when you have subtraction, especially if you

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: want to guarantee that the output of the subtraction is going to be positive. So if you have two values

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: x and y, and you know algebraically that you can prove that x is always greater than y,

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: in a computer you may actually find that x minus y ends up being negative. So if you rely it on

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: x minus y being positive in subsequent steps of your program, you could run into trouble.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: One case where you see this a lot is computing the variance. So there's one way that you can write

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the variance where it's the expectation of a squared value. If you implement the variance this

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: way in your code, it's always going to give you positive numbers because the output of the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the squaring value will always be non negative. I shouldn't say positive, sorry.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And then if you look at the difference between the expectation of the squared value of the function

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: and the square of the expected value of the function, algebraically that's a perfectly

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: valid way of rearranging this expression for the variance. And a lot of times this is actually the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: one where we actually have access to those values to compute it. Unfortunately it's numerically

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: unstable because if we have a little bit of rounding error on the left and a little bit of

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: rounding error on the right and these values were very close to zero to start with, if the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: rounding error goes in the wrong direction, we can end up with a small negative value instead of zero.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And that can cause trouble especially if you were planning to pass this value to a square root

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: or a logarithm later on. Logger them in square root are also functions where you want to be very

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: careful. The logarithm of zero is negative infinity and the logarithm of a negative number is imaginary

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: usually in software frameworks like NumPy we end up treating that as a not a number.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The square root of zero is zero so that's actually safe but the derivative of square root has a

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: divide by zero if you take the derivative at x equals zero. So you definitely want to avoid underflow

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: or rounding to a negative value in the argument to either log or square root.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: One very common place where this comes up is when we compute the standard deviation.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The standard deviation is the square root of the variance. So if you compute the variance using

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: the dangerous strategy from this slide, you can end up with this rounding to a small negative number

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: and then you take the square root of that small negative number and run into trouble.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: The logarithm of x is a common pattern that comes up a lot in deep learning. We often see this

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: when we're computing log probabilities and the probability is based on taking the x of some

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: argument. Whenever you see this you should simplify it to just the value x.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: Fiona will do this for you automatically a lot of frameworks don't.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: And if you do this you can avoid overflowing the x because you just take x rather than

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: computing x of x first before competing the log. You can also avoid underflow in x which

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: would then cause the logarithm to turn into negative.

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: So now I have a little bit of an exercise for you. Let's say that we're doing a little bit of a

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: hack to avoid a divide by zero. We want to take a normalized, we want to compute a normalized

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: version of x where x will have unit standard deviation and we divide x by some estimate of the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: standard deviation in order to do that. We know that our standard deviation might have some

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: round to zero trouble. So we're going to add a small constant epsilon of 10 to the minus 7

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: to our standard deviation in order to make sure we don't divide by zero when we compute the

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: normalized version of x. So there's two different ways we can imagine doing this. One is we could

chunks.title: Lecture4.mp3
chunks.text: take the square root of epsilon plus the variance and use that as our standard deviation. The other
